title: CoT Sanity Monitor and Anomaly Dashboard

story.as_a: tenant operator wanting visibility into agents that might be cheating
story.i_want_to: have a non-blocking monitor that scans agent chain-of-thought output for known cheating markers ("let's hack", "bypass", "fake pass", etc) and surfaces matches on an anomaly dashboard
story.so_that: I can spot obvious gaming attempts without relying on scoring that degrades as models get smarter

rationale: Contemporary research finds that current-generation LLMs often literally narrate their cheating intentions in CoT. A weak keyword/regex scanner catches many such cases cheaply. The monitor is explicitly a soft signal that routes stories for deeper review, NOT a training signal — training against it would make it useless.

acceptance_criteria.description: mcp-server captures review-agent output (findings summary + any accessible CoT) and posts it to `POST /api/v1/reviews/:id/cot` as structured metadata (not the full CoT in production, to avoid storing sensitive reasoning).

Loopctl runs a keyword+regex scan against the posted content for markers: `let's hack`, `bypass`, `skip this test`, `hardcoded pass`, `not my problem`, `good enough`, `close enough`, `technically passes`, `just mark it done`, `will fix later`, `stub it out`. Initial list stored in a config with room for operator extension.

Matches are recorded in a new `cot_anomalies` table: id, tenant_id, story_id, review_record_id, matched_markers (array), matched_context_snippet (truncated to 200 chars), severity (enum: low/medium/high based on marker), detected_at.
An audit_chain entry is written for each anomaly with action `cot_anomaly` and the matched markers. The full CoT content is NOT written to the audit chain.
LiveView at `/admin/anomalies` lists recent anomalies with story context, matched markers, and action buttons (dismiss as false positive, flag story for deeper review, revoke reviewer's dispatch).
Review agents are NOT told about the monitor (no tool description mentioning it, no error message acknowledging it). This preserves the signal from gaming.

A private system article (viewable only by operators with role `:superadmin`, NOT published to the public wiki) documents the monitor rationale and the marker list. Public system articles do not mention the monitor.
Migration for `cot_anomalies` table. CHANGELOG entry. Tenant isolation: anomalies are scoped by tenant.

test_cases.name: Known marker in posted CoT triggers anomaly record
Operator dismisses an anomaly as false positive

technical_notes: Marker matching: use Regex.compile with case-insensitive flag, combined with Erlang's :binary.match for speed.
The markers list lives in config/config.exs under `:loopctl, :cot_markers` with an operator override path.

The private system article has scope: :system but an additional `visibility: :operator_only` flag — requires a new field on articles. Alternatively, ship it as a docs/ file in the repo instead of via the wiki, keeping the wiki fully public.
Do NOT add the monitor to any public documentation. The moment an agent knows it exists, the signal starts degrading.
